elements.sent-2411-001: Takyu and K.Ueda \institute{Waseda University, Tokyo 169-8555, Japan @ueda.info.waseda.ac.jp.}
elements.sent-2411-002: Hierarchical graph rewriting is a highly expressive computational formalism that manipulates \revise{graphs enhanced with box structures for representing hierarchies.}
elements.sent-2411-003: It has provided the foundations of various graph-based modeling tools, but the design of high-level declarative languages based on hierarchical graph rewriting is still a challenge.
elements.sent-2411-004: For a solid design choice, well-established formalisms with backgrounds other than graph rewriting would provide useful guidelines.
elements.sent-2411-005: Proof nets of Multiplicative Exponential Linear Logic (MELL) is such a framework because its original formulation of cut elimination is essentially graph rewriting involving box structures, where so-called Promotion Boxes with an indefinite number of non-local edges may be cloned, migrated and deleted.
elements.sent-2411-006: This work builds on LMNtal as a declarative language based on hierarchical (port) graph rewriting, and discusses how it can be extended to support the above operations on Promotion Boxes of MELL proof nets.
elements.sent-2411-007: LMNtal thus extended turns out to be a practical graph rewriting language that has strong affinity with MELL proof nets.
elements.sent-2411-008: The language features provided are general enough to encode other well-established models of concurrency.
elements.sent-2411-009: Using the toolchain of LMNtal that provides state-space search and model checking, we implemented cut elimination rules of MELL proof nets in extended LMNtal and demonstrated that the platform could serve as a useful workbench for proof nets.
elements.sent-2411-010: Connectivity and hierarchy are the two major structuring mechanisms that often occur simultaneously in modeling various phenomena ranging from computing to human societies.
elements.sent-2411-011: In organizational charts and system configuration diagrams, connectivity is represented by lines, whereas the hierarchy of groups is represented by box-like structures.
elements.sent-2411-012: Because of their universality, it is desirable to have a computational model and a programming language that formally handles this structure which we call hierarchical graphs.
elements.sent-2411-013: There exist diverse formalisms for hierarchical graph rewriting \cite{berry_chemical_1992,milner_bigraphical_2001,ueda_lmntal_2009,drewes_hierarchical_2002,ene_attributed_2018,alves_new_2011,muroya_hypernet_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, designing a practical high-level declarative language based on hierarchical graph rewriting is still a challenge.
elements.sent-2411-015: Well-established formal systems potentially related to graph rewriting could provide guidelines of a solid design.
elements.sent-2411-016: Multiplicative Exponential Linear Logic (MELL) \cite{girard_linear_1987 is such a logical system related to hierarchical graph rewriting, in which Proof Nets was formulated as a formalism for representing proofs graphically, abstracting some of the symmetries in sequent-based proofs.}
elements.sent-2411-017: In particular, \newsentence{cut elimination rules for exponentials are represented as the migration, cloning, and deletion of box structures called Promotion Boxes.}
elements.sent-2411-018: In this study, we start with the hierarchical (port) graph rewriting language LMNtal \cite{ueda_lmntal_2009 and refine its language constructs to establish strong affinity with the operations on Proof Nets.}
elements.sent-2411-019: We build on LMNtal because it is formalized in the standard style of programming languages with abstract syntax and structural operational semantics.
elements.sent-2411-020: It also allows interpretation as a logic programming language based on intuitionistic linear logic \cite{ueda_lmntal_2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: An encoding of MELL Proof Nets using the original constructs of LMNtal was outlined in a poster \cite{takyu_encoding_2023 , showing a promising direction, but we show that the operations on Proof Nets can be fully supported only with newly designed and implemented constructs.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Formalization of graph cloning and deletion has been a challenge also in the (mainstream) algebraic approach of graph rewriting \cite{corradini_sesqui-pushout_2006,overbeek_graph_2021,brenas_verifying_2018 , but the main theme of the present work is to introduce the constructs for graph cloning and deletion through boxes into a practical declarative language.}
elements.sent-2411-023: The main contributions of this paper are threefold.
elements.sent-2411-024: Firstly, we extend the hierarchical graph rewriting language LMNtal to make it possible to describe the operations of Promotion Boxes in MELL Proof Nets, define operations corresponding to the duplication and deletion of Promotion Box, and implement them (Sect.~\ref{section: ext ).}
elements.sent-2411-025: Secondly, we demonstrate that the extended LMNtal serves as a workbench for Proof Nets (Sect.~\ref{section: encoding-pn ).}
elements.sent-2411-026: Thirdly, we demonstrate that the proposed constructs are sufficiently general by showing an encoding of the Ambient Calculus, a model of concurrency with box-like structures (Sect.~\ref{section: encoding-proc ).}
elements.section-2411-002: LMNtal: A Hierarchical Graph Rewriting Language
elements.sent-2411-027: We briefly explain the hierarchical graph rewriting language LMNtal \cite{ueda_lmntal_2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: LMNtal consists of (i) term-based syntax, (ii) structural congruence on terms that provides interpretation of terms as graphs, and (iii) small-step reduction relation.
elements.sent-2411-029: For lack of space, we skip detailed syntactic conditions of (i) and introduce (ii) and (iii) informally, and give further details in Appendix~\ref{sec:more_LMNtal .}
elements.sent-2411-030: A tutorial introduction to LMNtal can be found in \cite{LMNtal_tutorial and the full formal definition in \cite{ueda_lmntal_2009 .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: The syntax of LMNtal is given in Fig.~\ref{fig:lmntal-syntax , where \newsentence{three syntactic categories, link names (denoted by $X$), atom names (denoted by $p$), and possibly empty membrane names (denoted by $m$), are presupposed.}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \vspace*{-.3em} \[ \begin{array}{rcrccccccccl} (process) P ::= \zero \bigm| p\paren{X_1 \pc \ldots \pc X_n} \bigm| P \pc P \bigm| m\mem{P} \bigm| T \react T [3pt] (process template) T ::= \zero \bigm| p\paren{X_1 \pc \ldots \pc X_n} \bigm| T \pc T \bigm| m\mem{T} \bigm| T \react T [1pt] \bigm| \verb+@+ p \bigm| \multicolumn{7}{c}{ \$ p\bracket{X_1 \pc \ldots \pc X_n | A} \bigm| p \paren{*X_1 \pc \ldots \pc *X_n}} [3pt] (residual) A ::= \verb+[]+ \bigm| \multicolumn{4}{l}{* X} \end{array} \] \caption{Syntax of LMNtal.} \label{fig:lmntal-syntax}
elements.sent-2411-032: Since LMNtal was originally developed as a model of concurrency.
elements.sent-2411-033: Occurrences of a link name represent endpoints of a one-to-one link between atoms (or more precisely, atom arguments).
elements.sent-2411-034: For this purpose, each link name in a process $P$ is allowed to occur at most twice (Link Condition).
elements.sent-2411-035: A link whose name occurs only once in $P$ is called a free link of $P$.
elements.sent-2411-036: Links may cross membranes and connect atoms located at different ``places'' of the membrane hierarchy.
elements.sent-2411-037: A graph in which each node has its own arity and totally ordered links, like an LMNtal graph, is often called a port graph.
elements.sent-2411-038: Process templates on both sides of a rewrite rule allow process contexts, rule contexts, and aggregates \cite{ueda_lmntal_2005,ueda_lmntal_2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-039: A process context, denoted $\verb+$+p[X_1 \pc \ldots \pc X_n | A]\ (n \geq 0) $, works as a wildcard that matches ``the rest of the processes'' (except rewrite rules \newsentence{that are matched by a rule context $@p$}) within the membrane in which it appears.
elements.sent-2411-040: The arguments specify what free link names may or must occur.
elements.sent-2411-041: When the residual $A$ is of the form $\verb+*+X$ (bundle), links other than $X_1 \pc \ldots \pc X_n$ may occur free, and $\verb+*+X$ stands for the sequence of those optional free links.
elements.sent-2411-042: When $A$ is of the form \verb+[+\verb+]+ \newsentence{ (in which case the ``|[]'' may be omitted) , no other free links may occur.}
elements.sent-2411-043: An aggregate, denoted $p\paren{*X_1 \pc \ldots \pc *X_n}\ (n > 0)$, represents a multiset of atoms with the name $p$, whose multiplicity coincides with the number of links represented by the argument bundles, denoted $|*X_i|$.
elements.sent-2411-044: The detailed syntactic conditions on aggregates are given in \cite{ueda_lmntal_2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Rewrite rules must observe several syntactic conditions \cite{ueda_lmntal_2009 so that the Link Condition is preserved in the course of program execution.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Most importantly, link names in a rewrite rule must occur exactly twice, and each process context must occur exactly once at the top level of distinct cells in the LHS of a rule.
elements.sent-2411-047: A rewrite rule repeatedly acts on an LMNtal graph located in the same ``place'' of the membrane hierarchy.

elements.subsection-2411-003: Extension of LMNtal Syntax: Aggregates of Process Contexts
elements.sent-2411-085: We propose an extension of LMNtal that solves the above issues and provides constructs for cloning and deleting membranes with indefinitely many free links.
elements.sent-2411-086: First, we extend the syntax of process templates to allow aggregates of process contexts (in addition to atom aggregates): \begin{equation}{cccccc} T ::= \ldots ~\bigm|~ \$p[*X_1\pc *X_2\pc \ldots \pc *X_n] \quad (n>0) \end{equation} where each $*X_i$ is a bundle that appears in the process context with the same name, \newsentence{which implies that $|*X_1 | = |*X_2 | = \ldots = |*X_n |$, where $|*X_i |$ stands for the number of links \newsentence{represented by $*X_i$ upon the present rewriting.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: This extends the notion of aggregates to a process context, representing $|*X_i|$ process contexts each with $n$ free links.
elements.sent-2411-088: As a practical decision, this functionality is provided as an API as was the case of $nlmem$.
